items.foco: Rome IV criteria for functional constipation
items.title: Rome IV Criteria
items.contentHtml:
Rome IV criteria for functional constipation

  • Must be met during the last 3 months with onset of symptoms at least 6 months before diagnosis.
  • Includes 2 or more of the following: straining during >25% of bowel movements, hard or goaty stools (Bristol 1-2) in >25%, feeling of incomplete evacuation in >25%, manual maneuvers to facilitate defecation in >25%, <3 spontaneous bowel movements per week.
  • Loose bowel movements rarely occur without the use of laxatives.
  • Criteria for irritable bowel syndrome are not met.

items.title: Constipation from opioids and naloxegol
items.contentHtml:
Opioid constipation: management with naloxegol

  • Opioid-induced constipation is mediated by mu receptors in the myenteric plexus, reducing intestinal motility and secretion.
  • Naloxegol is a peripheral mu receptor antagonist (PAMORA) with dose of 12.5 mg or 25 mg per day; It does not cross the blood-brain barrier, so it does not reverse analgesia.
  • Indicated in constipation refractory to osmotic laxatives in patients with chronic non-cancer pain or stable cancer.
  • Adverse effects: abdominal pain, diarrhea, nausea; Contraindicated if intestinal obstruction.

items.foco: Fecaloma: indication for enema or manual extraction
items.title: Fecaloma: management
items.contentHtml:
Fecaloma: indication for enema or manual extraction

  • The fecaloma is a mass of hard impacted feces that cannot be evacuated spontaneously; It is usually located in the rectum.
  • First measure: phosphate enema or mineral oil enema (night retention) to soften the impaction.
  • If the enema fails, manual digital extraction is indicated under analgesia and with careful technique to avoid rectal or vagal perforation.
  • In the elderly, fecal impaction can present as overflow diarrhea. (pseudo-diarrhea), which is a diagnostic trap.

items.foco: Dietary fiber: gradual increase and its effect on the colon
items.title: Dietary fiber
items.contentHtml:
Dietary fiber: gradual increase and its effect on the colon

  • Soluble fiber (psyllium, glucomannan) is fermented in the colon producing short chain fatty acids that stimulate motility; Insoluble fiber (bran) increases fecal volume due to water retention.
  • The increase should be gradual (start with 5-10 g/day and go up to 25-30 g/day) to avoid bloating and flatulence.
  • In chronic constipation fiber is effective if there is no pelvic floor dyssynergia; in patients with slow transit it can worsen distension.
  • Requires simultaneous intake of at least 1.5-2 liters of fluid per day to avoid bezoar formation.

items.foco: Osmotic laxatives: polyethylene glycol as first line
items.title: Osmotic laxatives and polyethylene glycol
items.contentHtml:
Osmotic laxatives: polyethylene glycol as first line

  • Polyethylene glycol (PEG) is a non-absorbable osmotic laxative that retains water in the intestinal lumen, increasing fecal volume and stimulating peristalsis.
  • Dose for chronic constipation: 17 g dissolved in 240 ml of water once a day day, adjustable according to response.
  • It is first line in chronic functional constipation due to its effectiveness and safety profile (it does not produce electrolyte imbalance, unlike lactulose at high doses).
  • Lactulose (10-20 g/day) is an alternative, but it can cause flatulence and hypernatremia in elderly people with kidney failure.

items.title: IBS-C: diagnosis and management
items.contentHtml:
Irritable bowel syndrome with predominant constipation

  • It is defined by recurrent abdominal pain at least 1 day per week in the last 3 months, associated with 2 or more of: relationship with defecation, change in frequency of bowel movements or change in consistency (Rome IV).
  • In the constipation-predominant subtype (IBS-C), abnormal stools are Bristol 1-2 in >25% of stools and Bristol 6-7 in <25%.
  • First line: soluble fiber (psyllium) and antispasmodics (hyoscine) for pain.
  • Refractory: linaclotide (guanylate cyclase C agonist) 290 μg/day or lubiprostone (chloride channel activator) 24 μg twice a day.

items.title: Secondary causes of constipation
items.contentHtml:
Secondary causes: hypothyroidism, hypercalcemia, diabetes

  • Hypothyroidism: reduces intestinal motility due to decreased thyroid stimulation on smooth muscle; It is associated with intestinal myxedema in severe cases.
  • Hypercalcemia: inhibits smooth muscle contraction and reduces the release of acetylcholine; serum calcium >12 mg/dl is usually symptomatic.
  • Diabetes mellitus: autonomic neuropathy that affects the myenteric plexus, causing slow transit; It may coexist with diarrhea due to bacterial overgrowth.
  • Other causes: Parkinson's disease, scleroderma, drugs (opioids, anticholinergics, calcium antagonists).

items.foco: Alarm: bleeding, weight loss, anemia, onset >50 years
items.title: Warning signs of constipation
items.contentHtml:
Alarm: bleeding, weight loss, anemia, onset >50 years old

  • The warning signs require ruling out colorectal cancer through colonoscopy.
  • Rectal bleeding (no obvious hemorrhoids), involuntary weight loss, iron deficiency anemia and onset of symptoms after 50 years are high-risk indicators.
  • Others: family history of colorectal cancer or inflammatory bowel disease, palpable abdominal mass, recent change in bowel habit.
  • In patients >50 years of age with new constipation and no alarm, screening with colonoscopy is recommended according to prevention guidelines.

items.foco: Anorectal manometry for pelvic floor dyssynergia
items.title: Anorectal manometry and dyssynergia
items.contentHtml:
Anorectal manometry for pelvic floor dyssynergia

  • Pelvic floor dyssynergia is an incoordination between abdominal contraction and relaxation of the anal sphincter during defecation.
  • Anorectal manometry measures pressures at rest and during effort; a dyssynergic pattern shows paradoxical increase in anal sphincter pressure when pushing.
  • It is confirmed with ejection balloon (time >1 minute to expel a balloon with 50 ml of water) and defecography or dynamic magnetic resonance imaging.
  • Treatment: biofeedback to reeducate coordination; laxatives are ineffective if the dyssynergia is not corrected.

items.foco: Lubiprostone and linaclotide in refractory chronic constipation
items.title: Lubiprostone and linaclotide
items.contentHtml:
Lubiprostone and linaclotide in refractory chronic constipation

  • Lubiprostone is an activator of type 2 chloride channels (CIC-2) in the intestinal epithelium, which increases fluid secretion and accelerates transit; dose: 24 μg twice a day.
  • Linaclotide is a guanylate cyclase C receptor agonist that increases the secretion of chlorine and bicarbonate, softening stools; dose: 290 μg once a day (for IBS-C) or 145 μg (for functional chronic constipation).
  • Both are indicated in chronic constipation refractory to osmotic laxatives.
  • Main adverse effect: diarrhea (up to 20% with linaclotide); Contraindicated in intestinal obstruction.

items.foco: Fecal impaction in the elderly: prevention with fiber and fluids
items.title: Fecal impaction in the elderly
items.contentHtml:
Fecal impaction in the elderly: prevention with fiber and fluids

  • In the elderly, fecal impaction is common due to immobility, polypharmacy (opioids, anticholinergics) and decreased rectal sensation.
  • Prevention: dietary fiber 25-30 g/day (prefer soluble fiber to avoid distention) and fluid intake 1.5-2 L/day if there is no cardiac or renal contraindication.
  • Scheduled use of polyethylene glycol (17 g/day) or lactulose (15-30 ml/day) as prophylaxis in patients at high risk.
  • Avoid chronic stimulant laxatives (senna, bisacodyl) due to the risk of colonic atony and imbalance electrolytic.

items.foco: Adverse effects of stimulant laxatives: avoidable chronic use
items.title: Stimulant laxatives: adverse effects
items.contentHtml:
Adverse effects of stimulant laxatives: avoidable chronic use

  • Stimulant laxatives (senna, bisacodyl, sodium picosulfate) act on the submucosal plexus, increasing motility and secretion.
  • Chronic use (>6-12 months) can cause colonic atony (cathartic colon), hydroelectrolyte imbalance (hypokalemia, hypocalcemia) and melanosis coli (deposition of lipofuscin in the mucosa, reversible upon suspension).
  • melanosis coli is a benign endoscopic finding, but it can hide polyps; It is not associated with an increased risk of colorectal cancer.
  • Its use is recommended only in the short term or rescue, not as a first line in chronic constipation.
